nodes.original_text_name: American Anti-Communist Alliance (also AAA or Triple A)
nodes.context: Far-right paramilitary organization that conducted terror campaign against Colombian communists in 1978-1979.
Paramilitary organization formed in 1981-1982 by Medellín Cartel, military, and elites to fight guerrillas.

nodes.standard_name: Asociación Campesina de Ganaderos y Agricultores del Magdalena Medio
nodes.original_text_name: Asociación Campesina de Ganaderos y Agricultores del Magdalena Medio (ACDEGAM)
nodes.context: Legal front for paramilitary groups, handling logistics and public relations, promoting anti-labor policies.
Political movement formed by members of ACDEGAM in August 1989, seen as legitimizing paramilitarism.
Private army hired by Fidel Castaño, based at Las Tangas ranch, later became ACCU.

nodes.standard_name: Autodefensas Campesinas de Córdoba y Uraba
nodes.original_text_name: Autodefensas Campesinas de Córdoba y Uraba (ACCU)
aliases: Peasant Self-Defense Forces of Córdoba and Urabá
nodes.context: Paramilitary group formed in 1994 from Los Tangueros, worked with military to crush guerrillas.
Drug trafficking organization involved in forming MAS.
Military force linked to Triple A and other paramilitaries.
Marxist-Leninist guerrilla group, target of paramilitaries.
Communist guerrilla group that demobilized in 1990.
Umbrella paramilitary organization in Colombia, formed in 1997, involved in drug trade and counterinsurgency.
Colombian drug cartel led by Pablo Escobar, active in cocaine trafficking.
Colombian drug cartel, rival of Medellín Cartel, involved in cocaine trafficking.
Paramilitary organization formed to hunt Pablo Escobar, led by Fidel Castaño.
Paramilitary group in Colombia, partnered with Colombian Navy in Barrancabermeja.

nodes.standard_name: Servicios Especiales De Vigilancia y Seguridad Privada
nodes.original_text_name: Servicios Especiales De Vigilancia y Seguridad Privada
nodes.context: Legal paramilitary groups authorized in 1994, worked with military and illegal paramilitaries.
Marxist–Leninist guerrilla group in Colombia, opposed by paramilitaries.
Right-wing paramilitary umbrella group in Colombia, involved in drug trade and human rights abuses.
Dominant criminal and paramilitary organization in Colombia in the early 2010s.
Dominant criminal and paramilitary organization in Colombia in the early 2010s.
Dominant criminal and paramilitary organization in Colombia; by late 2017 the only one with a national presence.
Dominant criminal and paramilitary organization in Colombia in the early 2010s.
Dominant criminal and paramilitary organization in Colombia in the early 2010s.
Dominant criminal and paramilitary organization in Colombia in the early 2010s.
Dominant criminal and paramilitary organization in Colombia in the early 2010s.
Dominant criminal and paramilitary organization in Colombia in the early 2010s.
Dominant criminal and paramilitary organization in Colombia in the early 2010s.
Dominant criminal and paramilitary organization in Colombia in the early 2010s.
Dominant criminal and paramilitary organization in Colombia in the early 2010s.
Dominant criminal and paramilitary organization in Colombia in the early 2010s.
Leftist guerrilla group in Colombia, involved in drug trade and conflict with paramilitaries.
Drug cartel that collapsed in the 1990s, creating an opening for paramilitaries.
Drug cartel that collapsed in the 1990s, creating an opening for paramilitaries.
A right-wing paramilitary group responsible for killing thousands of Colombian civilians.

edges.evidence_quote: It was later revealed that the organization had direct links to the Colombian National Army.
edges.context: Triple A had direct links to the Colombian National Army.

edges.evidence_quote: Between the end of 1981 and the beginning of 1982, members of the Medellín Cartel, the Colombian military, ... formed a paramilitary organization known as Muerte a Secuestradores
edges.context: Medellín Cartel members were part of forming MAS.

edges.target: Asociación Campesina de Ganaderos y Agricultores del Magdalena Medio
edges.evidence_quote: ACDEGAM worked to promote anti-labor policies and threatened anyone involved with organizing for labor or peasants' rights. The threats were backed by the MAS
edges.context: MAS backed ACDEGAM's threats with violence.

edges.source: Asociación Campesina de Ganaderos y Agricultores del Magdalena Medio
edges.evidence_quote: In August 1989, the Movimiento de Restauración Nacional (MORENA) was formed by members of ACDEGAM.
edges.context: MORENA was formed by members of ACDEGAM.

edges.target: Autodefensas Campesinas de Córdoba y Uraba
edges.evidence_quote: In 1994, Carlos took control of Los Tangueros, which officially changed their name to the Autodefensas Campesinas de Córdoba y Uraba (ACCU).
edges.context: Los Tangueros became ACCU in 1994.

edges.source: Autodefensas Campesinas de Córdoba y Uraba
edges.evidence_quote: The ACCU began networking with other paramilitary groups such as the MAS
edges.context: ACCU networked with MAS.

edges.source: Autodefensas Campesinas de Córdoba y Uraba
edges.evidence_quote: The ACCU began working with regional military forces, such as the Bomboná battalion
edges.context: ACCU worked with military forces like Bomboná battalion.

edges.evidence_quote: members of the Medellín Cartel, the Colombian military, ... formed a paramilitary organization known as Muerte a Secuestradores
edges.context: Colombian military members were part of forming MAS.

edges.source: Autodefensas Campesinas de Córdoba y Uraba
edges.evidence_quote: The ACCU began working with regional military forces ... to crush the guerrillas
edges.context: ACCU fought against FARC-EP guerrillas.

edges.evidence_quote: They formed this organization to defend their economic interests, to fight against the guerrillas
edges.context: MAS was formed to fight against guerrillas including FARC.

edges.evidence_quote: The Calí Cartel provided $50 million to pay for weapons, informants, and assassins, with the hopes that they could wipe out their primary rival in the cocaine business.
edges.context: Cali Cartel funded Los Pepes to target Medellín Cartel.

edges.evidence_quote: the Calí Cartel, dissidents within the Medellín Cartel, and the MAS worked together to create a new paramilitary organization known as Perseguidos Por Pablo Escobar
edges.context: MAS collaborated with Cali Cartel and Medellín Cartel dissidents to form Los Pepes.

edges.evidence_quote: dissidents within the Medellín Cartel, and the MAS worked together to create a new paramilitary organization known as Perseguidos Por Pablo Escobar
edges.context: Dissidents from Medellín Cartel helped form Los Pepes to target Pablo Escobar.

edges.evidence_quote: the Calí Cartel provided $50 million to pay for weapons, informants, and assassins, with the hopes that they could wipe out their primary rival in the cocaine business.
edges.context: Cali Cartel funded Los Pepes to eliminate their rival Medellín Cartel.

edges.source: Servicios Especiales De Vigilancia y Seguridad Privada
edges.evidence_quote: Many of the CONVIVIR groups simply joined up with the Autodefensas Unidas de Colombia (AUC).
edges.context: CONVIVIR groups merged into the AUC.

edges.evidence_quote: As a response, the AUC engaged in a renewed series of massacres and assassinations, often with the passive or active aid of elements of the Colombian government's security forces, according to human rights organizations.
edges.context: AUC fought against FARC-EP as part of counterinsurgency.

edges.evidence_quote: BACRIMs continue to be involved in the drug trade, commit widespread human rights abuses, engage in forced displacement, and undermine democratic legitimacy in other ways—both in collusion with and opposition to FARC-EP guerrillas.
edges.context: BACRIMs (successor groups to AUC) both cooperate and oppose FARC-EP.

edges.evidence_quote: In the early 2010s, the Black Eagles, Los Rastrojos, Los Urabeños, Los Paisas, Los Machos, Renacer, Los Gaitanistas, Nueva Generación, Bloque Meta, Libertadores del Vichada, the ERPAC, and The Office of Envigado comprised the dominant criminal and paramilitary organizations.
edges.context: Black Eagles is listed as a dominant paramilitary group alongside AUC successor groups.

edges.evidence_quote: In the early 2010s, the Black Eagles, Los Rastrojos, Los Urabeños, Los Paisas, Los Machos, Renacer, Los Gaitanistas, Nueva Generación, Bloque Meta, Libertadores del Vichada, the ERPAC, and The Office of Envigado comprised the dominant criminal and paramilitary organizations.
edges.context: Los Rastrojos is listed as a dominant paramilitary group alongside AUC successor groups.

edges.evidence_quote: The downfall of the Medellín and Cali Cartels in the 1990s created an opening for paramilitary groups, which controlled northern Colombia (the key transnational smuggling route), to take over the international cocaine trade.
edges.context: The collapse of the Medellín Cartel allowed paramilitary groups like the AUC to take over the cocaine trade.
